method3.generated_article:
==Geography==
Kavachi is a shallow submarine stratovolcano in the South Pacific Ocean, located in the western Solomon Islands about 24 km south of Vangunu Island.[2] It lies in the Pacific Ring of Fire at a convergent plate boundary where the Pacific Plate subducts beneath the Solomon Plate, a setting associated with frequent volcanic activity.[2][3] The volcano rises more than 1,200 m from the seafloor, and its summit is close to the sea surface, lying roughly 20–24 m below sea level during surveyed conditions.[2][5] Its edifice is roughly conical, with flanks descending to depths greater than 1,000 m and slopes of about 18°.[4][5]  

Bathymetric observations describe the main summit as an oblong, pockmarked crater measuring approximately 120 m by 75 m, with the crater rim at an average depth of about 24 m.[5] A secondary summit or southwest extension has been identified about 1.3 km southwest of the main summit, separated from it by a narrow col.[5] Because Kavachi is shallow, eruptions can disturb the sea surface, produce discoloured water plumes visible from aircraft and satellites, and occasionally build short-lived islands above sea level.[2][3][5] These ephemeral islands have formed repeatedly during eruptive episodes but are commonly removed or submerged by wave erosion and renewed volcanic activity.[3][4]

==Eruptions==
Kavachi is among the most frequently active volcanoes in the Pacific, with near-surface eruptive episodes recorded at intervals of a few years since its first historically documented eruption in 1939.[1] The Global Volcanism Program has counted 33 historical eruptions, and the volcano has been active in roughly half of the years covered by its historical record, although early activity was probably underreported because of its remote setting.[1]

Eruptions commonly eject ash, incandescent lava blocks, gas, and volcanic debris through the sea surface.[2] Since 1939, eruptions have built temporary islands at least nine times, but these deposits have repeatedly been too small and poorly consolidated to survive wave erosion for more than a few months.[1] In late 1951, activity at Kavachi was associated with frequent small tsunamis, with one cited report giving a possible date of 22 December 1951.[1]

A renewed island-building episode was observed from the research vessel *Franklin* on 14 May 2000, when explosions threw ash and incandescent lava blocks up to 70 m above sea level and produced sulfurous steam plumes rising to about 500 m.[2] During activity in late 2001 and early 2002, explosive eruptions occurred daily from 27 November to 13 December, and on 13 January observers reported volcanic debris, gas bubbles, and frequent loud noises.[2] In 2003, an eruptive cycle formed an island about 15 m high, but by 16 November the summit had subsided or eroded to about 32 m below sea level, and no activity was then observed for the first time in four years.[2] Activity was again reported on 15 March 2004 after a period of quiescence.[2]

Kavachi’s eruptions are basaltic to mafic and are partly driven by volatile degassing.[4] A lull in January 2015 allowed researchers to enter and survey the active crater area, an opportunity that is normally prevented by the volcano’s explosive behavior.[3] During that inactive interval, scientists recorded warm-water and carbon dioxide venting, hydrothermal plume activity, and diffuse-flow venting from a secondary peak, showing that degassing and hydrothermal activity continued even in the absence of visible eruption.[3][4] As of May 2026, the most recent eruptive period was reported to have begun in 2021 and to be continuing.[1]

==Marine life==
Kavachi has become widely known for observations of marine organisms in its crater waters, leading to the popular nickname “Sharkcano” or “Sharkano”.[2][5] During quiescent periods, researchers have recorded sharks and fish in the volcano’s acidic, mineral-rich waters.[2] A 2015 expedition documented an ecosystem in the crater that included gelatinous zooplankton, reef fish, microbial communities, and sharks.[4][5] The shark species reported from the crater include the scalloped hammerhead shark (*Sphyrna lewini*) and the silky shark (*Carcharhinus falciformis*).[4]

The crater environment is considered extreme because volcanic gases and hydrothermal activity produce warm, acidic, particle-rich water with elevated concentrations of carbon dioxide and sulfur compounds.[4] Measurements reported in the crater included temperatures reaching about 42 °C, roughly 13 °C above surrounding waters, and a pH estimated at about 6.1.[4] The suspended-particle hydrothermal plume may support microbial communities, including sulfur-oxidizing bacteria such as *Sulfurimonas*, which can use sulfur and carbon dioxide in chemosynthetic processes.[4]

Scientists remain uncertain whether the sharks reside in the crater continuously or enter and leave according to changes in volcanic activity.[1][5] Proposed explanations for their presence include behavioral avoidance of eruptive phases, sensitivity to chemical or seismic warning cues, and physiological tolerance of warmer and more acidic water.[1] The observations have
